shot.subject: field
shot.notes: the grass ocean at dawn, wind drawing long silver waves across it

segments.text: It has been in every frame of this series, and the star of none. It hid the ants, built the worms' soil, and gave this world the very name of "the garden." But it is not scenery. It belongs to one of the most successful plant families that has ever lived.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: plunge from above the canopy down the full height of one blade

segments.text: Not the creatures among the towers. The towers.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: a single blade fills frame, ribbed and translucent in the light

segments.text: The grass — family Poaceae. Wheat, rice, maize, barley, sugarcane, bamboo: all of it is grass, and roughly half of every human meal begins in a blade shaped like this one.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: a long, fast weave between the blades, canopy light strobing

segments.text: Grasses are monocots — one of the two great branches of the flowering plants. You can read it in the leaf: parallel veins running the length of the blade, never the branching net of an oak leaf. Simple, cheap, and repeated ten million times across this field.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: straight down: the canopy a dense, seething green texture

segments.text: From above, individuality vanishes into fabric. But every thread of that fabric is a complete organism running the same chemistry the great trees run — turning sunlight, water, and air into sugar — packed into a leaf the thickness of paper.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: camera at soil level, looking up a blade to the sky, wind rocking it

segments.text: And here is the trick that made the family a global empire. Most plants grow from their tips. Cut the top off a grass, and it does not care.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: extreme macro at the base of a blade, where the pale new growth pushes up

segments.text: The growing zones of a grass sit low, near the soil, tucked at the base of the leaf. Botanists call them intercalary meristems. Bite off the top, mow it, burn it, and the blade simply pushes up again from below — good as new.

shot.subject: soldier
shot.notes: the Visitor stands among the blades as a shadow of some grazer passes over

segments.text: This is no accident. Grasses and grazing animals grew up together, each shaping the other over millions of years. The herds that would kill an ordinary plant merely trim a grass — and in return, the grass feeds the herd. It is the one plant built to be eaten and thank you for it.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: light glinting off the hard, glassy edge of a blade

segments.text: It defends itself, too, but selectively. Grass leaves are laced with tiny grains of silica — literally microscopic glass, the same mineral as sand. That grit wears down the teeth of anything that eats too much, and it is why a grazer's molars grow so tall and tough. Even the food fights back.

shot.subject: worm
shot.notes: below the soil line: a dense mat of fine roots, a worm working past them

segments.text: Now look down, because most of a grass is underground. Not a single taproot but a fibrous net — thousands of fine roots knitting through the soil, binding the Gardener's sculpted hills against wind and rain. Where grass grows, the soil stays. Where it dies, the soil blows away.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: roots gripping a crumbling edge of exposed earth, holding it together

segments.text: That root net is also one of the planet's great quiet stores of carbon. A grassland keeps most of its body below the surface, packing carbon into the soil year after year. The world's prairies and savannas hold vast reserves down there — an underground forest you never see.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: the whole meadow, every previous episode's cast implied within it

segments.text: Remove any animal from this series, and the garden would limp on. Remove the grass, and there is no garden — only bare, blowing soil, and roots enough are all that stand between a living field and a dust bowl.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: a slender seedhead at the tip of a stem, dangling pale anthers in the wind

segments.text: Grass flowers, but you have never stopped to admire one, because it is not built for you. No petals, no scent, no nectar, no color worth the name. Grass gave all that up.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: a gust crosses the seedheads; a haze of fine pollen lifts off the whole field

segments.text: It courts the wind instead. Those drab anthers dangle in the breeze and release clouds of dry pollen to drift on the air, some of it finding another grass flower by sheer arithmetic of numbers. No courier, no contract, no reward — just the wind, and enough pollen to make the odds pay. It is cheaper than beauty, and it built continents of prairie.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: a ripened seed at the stem's tip, ready to drop

segments.text: Every flower that catches becomes a seed — a packed lunch and a passenger, ready to ride an animal's fur, a bird's gut, or the same wind that made it, out into open ground to start the whole cycle again.

shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: several blade forms side by side: broad, fine, tall, arched

segments.text: Poaceae is enormous — thousands of species, from lawn blades to bamboo that can stand taller than the trees. Look closely and this field is not one grass but many, mixed like accents in a single language.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: rise slowly until variety blends back into that single rolling green

segments.text: Distance blends the accents into one voice — the hiss and rush of wind over ten million flexing blades. It is the sound of the most quietly dominant plant family on Earth, doing the thing it has always done: covering the ground, holding it, and feeding nearly everything that walks on it.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: evening light rakes the canopy; scattered blooms glow among the green

segments.text: But a nation of green has one loud exception — scattered through the towers, points of deliberate, spendthrift color that broadcast on a channel the grass never bothered to use.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: a bloom opens in the low light, petals arranged around a bright center

segments.text: Petals, scent, and nectar — an entire industry built to hire an animal. Next: the flowers.
